title: How a Compiler Works
description: Compilers translate high-level source code into low-level machine instructions or executables before running them.
codeBlock:
Source Code
  ↓ [Lexical Analysis (Lexer)] -> Tokens
  ↓ [Syntax Analysis (Parser)] -> AST
  ↓ [Semantic Analysis] -> Checked AST
  ↓ [Optimization] -> Optimized AST
  ↓ [Code Generation] -> Target Machine Code
details:
A compiler is a transformative program that converts human-readable source code into executable machine code through a sequence of well-defined phases. Unlike interpreters, which execute code incrementally, compilers perform a wholesale translation before execution, enabling extensive optimization and producing standalone binaries. This design choice underlies the performance characteristics of languages like C, C++, Rust, and Go, where compiled executables run at native speed without runtime interpretation overhead.

The compilation pipeline consists of several distinct stages: lexical analysis (tokenization), syntax analysis (parsing into an Abstract Syntax Tree), semantic analysis (type checking and validation), intermediate representation generation, optimization (both machine-independent and machine-specific), and final code generation. Each stage consumes the output of the previous one, forming a classic pipeline architecture. The Dragon Book (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman) remains the definitive reference on this architecture, first formalizing these concepts in 1986 and now in its second edition.

Modern compiler frameworks such as LLVM have revolutionized compiler development by providing reusable intermediate representations and optimization passes. LLVM's IR serves as a common substrate, enabling frontends for diverse languages — Clang for C/C++, rustc for Rust, swiftc for Swift — to share a single, aggressively optimizing backend targeting multiple architectures. For a visual walkthrough of each compilation stage, explore the Interactive Compiler Pipeline Guide.
